segments.s429-g49: بدو is the prepositional adverb 'to him/unto him' (to mark the indirect object). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3sg) — here used after بدو so literally 'to him said'. زال‌ای پسر is a vocative address ('Zāl—O son' or more naturally 'O son' with Zāl as the speaker's name understood); گوش دار is an imperative-like phrase where گوش = 'ear/attention' and دار is the light verb meaning 'have/keep' (classical/poetic usage = 'pay attention').
segments.s429-g50: سخن is 'the speech/these words' (object/topic). چون here links the following clause temporally ('as/when' in classical usage). به یاد آوری is a verbal phrase 'bring to mind / recall' where به + یاد (= 'to memory') and آوری is the verb 'you bring/recall' (present/subjunctive/second-person form in classical diction). هوش دار parallels گوش دار: هوش = 'awareness, wits' and دار = 'keep/possess' (i.e. 'be mindful').
segments.s429-g51: همه کارهای جهان is a genitive phrase 'all the deeds/affairs of the world'. را is the definite/object marker placed after that phrase for emphasis/topic. در here is the noun 'door/gate' (poetic use) and است is the copula 'is/are' (3sg), so the clause literally says that the world's affairs 'are (under) a door' — i.e. they share one gate/entrance.
segments.s429-g52: مگر is the exception marker 'except'. مرگ is 'death'. کانرا is a demonstrative/emphatic old form ('that one' / 'that of them') referring back to death. دری دیگر است literally 'is another door' (دری = archaic/poetic form of 'door' and دیگر = 'other'), so the clause contrasts death as having a different gate. Word order is verb-final (است) following the noun phrase.
segments.s429-g53: یکی چاره دانم is 'I know one remedy' (یکی = 'one', چاره = 'remedy/solution', دانم = 'I know' / 'I deem'). من is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. این را گزین is an imperative/command 'choose this' where را marks 'this' as the definite object and گزین is the imperative/short-form verb 'choose'.
segments.s429-g54: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. سیمرغ را is the object with را marking it definite. یار خوانم is a predicate where یار = 'companion/ally' and خوانم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/summon' (i.e. 'I shall call/summon as a companion'). برین is the fused form of بر + این (= بر این) meaning 'for this / to this end' (prepositional phrase of purpose). The verb comes at the end, consistent with classical word order.
segments.s429-g55: گر marks a conditional 'if'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. باشدم is an archaic/poetic optative/subjunctive form meaning roughly 'should be / may be / becomes' (classical usage where Modern Persian would use باشد or شود). زین سخن is an archaic fused form (زین = 'از این' = 'from/through this') meaning 'because of/through this speech/counsel'. رهنمای is a predicate noun 'guide' (so: 'if he be a guide because of this counsel').
segments.s429-g56: بماند is the verb 'remain' (3sg in this construction, here carrying future/optative sense after the condition 'if ...'). به ما is the dative 'to/for us'. کشور و بوم و جای is the triad 'country/realm and homeland and place/home' — the items that will remain. The clause order is verb + indirect object + list of things retained, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s429-g17: بدو گفت رستم is an inverted, poetic word order: literally “to him said Rostam” (= “Rostam said to him”). کزین غم چه سود is a short question: کزین = ک + این = “from this / about this,” غم “sorrow,” and چه سود “what benefit / what good?” — i.e. “what good is sorrow about this?”
segments.s429-g18: که introduces the explanatory clause (“for / because / that”). این ز آسمان بودنی کار بود literally: “this is a matter from heaven.” ز = poetic contraction of از “from,” آسمان = “heaven,” بودنی is a participial/adjectival form meaning “that-which-is / ordained” (i.e. belonging to being/heaven), and the final بود is the copula (was/is) used to assert the state.
segments.s429-g19: به پیش است کاری is again poetic order: literally “before (you) is a task.” به پیش = “ahead / before,” است = “is,” کاری = “a task / affair” (here the expected sense ‘task/affair’ is intended; the provided lexical senses did not include it). که دشوارتر is a relative clause “which is more difficult,” -تر marking the comparative.
segments.s429-g20: وزو جان من پر ز تیمارتر: وزو is a contracted/poetic form meaning roughly “from it / because of it” (here treated as an irregular/archaic form), جان من “my life,” پر ز = “full of,” and تیمارتر is comparative “more full of care/anxiety” (تیمار = care; -تر comparative). Together: “and my life is fuller of anxiety on account of it.”
segments.s429-g21: که هرچند من بیش پوزش کنم is a concessive clause: هرچند = “although / even though,” من ... پوزش کنم = “I should/might make excuses” with کنَم in the subjunctive/optative sense (possible/attempted action). بیش modifies پوزش “more / repeatedly.”
segments.s429-g22: که این شیردل را فروزش کنم: the second که introduces the content/purpose (“that / so that”); این شیردل را is the definite object “this lion‑hearted one” (را marks the direct object), فروزش = “radiance / splendor,” and کنم is again subjunctive/optative “I might make.” Grammatically it expresses attempting to augment or speak of the hero’s radiance.
segments.s429-g23: نجوید همی جز همه ناخوشی: نجوید (from جویدن/جست/جو) here functions like “will obtain / will find” in context (classical usage allows this sense), همی gives a habitual/emphatic flavor, جز = “except / but,” and همه ناخوشی = “complete misfortune / utter ill‑turn.” The phrase means “he will find nothing except complete misfortune.”
segments.s429-g24: به گفتار و کردار و گردنکشی lists the spheres in which the misfortune appears: به = “in / with / by,” گفتار = “speech,” کردار = “deeds / conduct,” and گردنکشی (literally “neck‑turning”) = “defiance / obstinacy.” The repeated و simply links the items in the list.
segments.s429-g25: رسیدم is a past first‑person singular verb ('I arrived'/'I went'); the subject 'I' is incorporated in the verb. ز هر سو is an ablative phrase ('from every side/every quarter') and به گرد جهان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('around the world') — both modify رسیدم to indicate the extent/scope of the movement or search. Word order (verb before adverbial in poetry) is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s429-g26: خبر یافتم is object + verb: خبر ('news/information') appears before یافتم ('I found/learned'), a common poetic order. ز here means 'from/about' introducing the source/realm of the news; آشکار و نهان form a coordinate phrase ('the manifest and the hidden') that specifies what kinds of news were found.
segments.s429-g27: گرفتم is past first‑person singular 'I took/seized' and governs the following noun phrase. کمربند دیو سپید is a noun + postmodifier chain ('the girdle of the white demon'); in classical poetic diction the ezāfe linking particle is often omitted, but the genitive/attributive relationship is understood.
segments.s429-g28: زدم is past 'I struck' (transitive); بر زمین ('on/upon the ground') is the locative complement. همچو introduces a simile ('like/as'); یک‑شاخ بید is the thing compared to ('a one‑trunk/one‑branched willow' — a poetic image). The object struck (the girdle) is understood from the previous segment.
segments.s429-g29: نتابم is an archaic/polar use of تابیدن with negative prefix (ن-) meaning 'I could not/barely could' or 'I would not endure/submit'; همی is an older continuous/emphatic particle ('still/indeed'). سر ز اسفندیار literally 'head from Esfandiyar' functions as the object/phrase indicating submission or yielding to Esfandiyar (i.e. 'bow my head to Esfandiyar').
segments.s429-g30: ازان is the archaic fused form of از آن ('from that'); زور ('strength') and آن بخشش ('that forbearance/forgiveness') are coordinated and governed by ازان, while کارزار ('battle') specifies the domain ('in battle'). The whole phrase gives the reason/cause why the speaker would not submit.
segments.s429-g31: خدنگم is 'my dagger' (خدنگ + possessive -م). ز سندان is an ablative phrase 'from the anvil.' گذر یافتی combines the noun گذر ('passage') with یافتن ('to find/obtain') to mean 'found passage' or 'was made to pass' — a noun+یافتن construction common in classical Persian used here to express that the dagger achieved/received passage from the anvil (i.e. was made to pass/penetrate).
segments.s429-g32: This is a counterfactual conditional: زبون ('abject/humbled') is the predicted result; داشتی‌گر is an archaic conditional form combining داشتی ('you would have had') with گر ('if'), i.e. 'had you had/if you had had'; سپر یافتی ('you had obtained a shield') is the protasis. Put together: 'You would have been humbled if you had obtained a shield.'
segments.s429-g1: وزان is a short poetic connector 'and/from that' (here: 'with that'); روی (face) is placed before the name as a preposed modifier meaning 'with that face/expression'. The verb رسید (past) follows the locative phrase به ایوان ('to the iwan/porch'), so the clause reads literally 'and with that face Rostam reached the iwan.'
segments.s429-g2: This clause is compact and somewhat elliptical. مر is an archaic/poetic indirect-object form ('to me' or 'for me' in classical usage) placed before the pronoun او ('him') and the object marker را. بران گونه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in that manner/like that', دستان ('hands') is the direct object, and بدید is the past tense verb 'saw' / 'showed' (contextually here meaning 'held out' or 'displayed'). Overall: '(to me) he (i.e. he/they) displayed his hands in that manner.'
segments.s429-g3: Two names are simply juxtaposed (زواره فرامرز) and followed by the compound past plural verb شدند ('became' / auxiliary here forming past passive/ intransitive result). گریان is an adjective/participial noun 'weeping/tears', so together the clause means 'Zovāreh and Faramarz were/grew weeping' = 'they wept.'
segments.s429-g4: ازان ('from that' / 'because of that') introduces the cause. خستگیهاش is a possessive plural 'his/their wearinesss' (خستگی + ها + -ش), where -ش refers back to the person previously mentioned; بریان is a predicate adjective 'burnt/consumed' used metaphorically, and شدند is the past plural copula/auxiliary 'they became/ were.' Thus: 'they were consumed by that weariness.'
segments.s429-g5: This is somewhat inverted word order typical of poetry. ز (poetic variant of از) + سربر ('from atop the head') gives a source phrase. همی کند is the present/imperfective auxiliary + verb 'keeps doing / was doing' (here continuous habitual: 'still does'), رودابه is the subject, and موی (hair) is the object. So: 'Rudabeh still arranges/lifts her hair from atop her head.'
segments.s429-g6: بر آواز ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'at/upon their voice(s)/call', همی gives the continuous/aspectual sense 'keeps/was', خست is an adjectival predicate 'tired/weary', and روی (face) names the affected part. The clause therefore means 'at their call her face became/grew weary.' Note the verbless predication: subject (Rudabeh) is understood from previous clause.
segments.s429-g7: زواره is the person/name; به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'quickly/soon'. گشادش is the verb/adjective 'opened/loosened it' where the -ش enclitic refers back to a possessed item (literally 'he/they opened his/its'), and میان here means 'middle/girdle/waist area' (poetic use). So the clause says 'Zovāreh quickly loosened/opened (it) at the middle' (i.e., opened his belt/garment).
segments.s429-g8: ازو 'from him' marks source. برکشیدند is the past plural 'they drew up/pulled forth' (commonly used for drawing a weapon). ببر بیان here is a compound name — the famous sword Babr-e Bayan — so both words function as the object (proper name) even though the provided senses list treats them as common nouns; the clause thus reads 'they drew from him the Babr-e Bayan.'
segments.s429-g9: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause ('whoever/anyone who'). دانا is an adjectival predicate 'wise' describing that person. بد here is likely a poetic/orthographic contraction of بود ('was') or an adverbial modifier; the text uses an archaic form, so the sense is uncertain. از کشورش is a prepositional phrase 'from his country' with the possessive enclitic ش attached to کشور.
segments.s429-g10: نشستند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb 'they sat' (here referring back to the people just mentioned). یکسر is an adverb 'at once/altogether' modifying نشستند; همه 'all/everyone' is the subject or resumed group. بر درش is a locative phrase 'at/on his door/gate' with the possessive enclitic ش attached to در (door). The word order is slightly archaic (verb before adverbial phrase).
segments.s429-g11: بفرمود is a polite past tense verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that/in order that'). رخش را is the definite object marked by را ('his steed' as the thing to be brought). پیش اوی is an archaic way of saying 'before him/in his presence' (پیش + the enclitic اوی).
segments.s429-g12: ببردند is a 3rd-person plural past 'they brought/took'. و links the clauses. هرکس که introduces another relative clause 'whoever who...'. بد here functions as an adverbial modifier meaning something like 'in a bad state' or 'desperately' (i.e., in dire need). چاره‌جوی is a compound noun 'seeker of remedy' or 'one seeking relief/help'.
segments.s429-g13: گرانمایه is an honorific adjective 'dear/esteemed/valuable' modifying دستان 'hands'. همی is an archaic/inchoative particle often used with verbs to indicate habitual or continuous action; کند is the verb 'does/makes' (here functioning as the past/habitual marker 'would do' with the narrative particle). موی is the direct object 'hair' (placed where the poet prefers).
segments.s429-g14: بران is an adverbial 'upon that/thereupon' linking to the previous action. خستگیها is a plural noun 'wearinesses/fatigue' giving the reason or state. بمالید is the past tense 'they rubbed/smeared' and روی here means 'face' (i.e., they rubbed their faces), following the verb as is typical in poetic word order.
segments.s429-g15: همی is the habitual/progressive particle ('kept/was'), and گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (the form is narrative/poetic). من زنده is a nominative phrase 'I, alive'; با پیر سر is an ablative or descriptive phrase 'with a gray/old head' (an idiom meaning 'with a hoary head' or 'old man'). Word order is emphatic and archaic.
segments.s429-g16: بدیدم is the 1st-person past 'I saw'. بدین سان is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in this manner/so' formed from the prefix ب + دین (an archaic demonstrative element) + سان 'manner'. گرامی پسر is a direct address or description 'honored/dear son'. The whole phrase reports what the speaker says he has seen.
segments.s429-g41: برستم is a past/perfective verb form placed before the subject for poetic force; here it means ‘I have escaped/rose free’. The personal pronoun من follows the verb (V–S order allowed in classical Persian). از + چنگ آن اژدها is a simple prepositional + noun phrase: از = 'from', چنگ = 'claw/grip', آن اژدها = 'that dragon'.
segments.s429-g42: ندانم is the negated present ‘I do not know’ (ن + دانم). کزین = from/through this (ک + زین fused). خسته آیم is a clause where خسته (‘weary/tired’) is predicative and آیم (1st‑person present/subjunctive of آمدن) expresses 'I come / I am'; together: 'I am/come weary'. رها at the end functions adjectivally ‘free/unbound’ — ‘freed’ — and links as the result state being questioned.
segments.s429-g43: چه اندیشم is an interrogative/optative clause: چه = 'what' and اندیشم is 1st‑person subjunctive/optative of 'to think' yielding 'what shall I think'. اکنون = 'now'. جزین نیست رای is the follow‑up: جزین = 'except this', نیست رای = 'there is no counsel/plan'. Note the topicalization: the exception (جزین) precedes the predicate.
segments.s429-g44: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). فردا = 'tomorrow'. بگردانم is 1st‑person subjunctive/future of گرداندن 'to turn/cause to move' meaning 'I will set (in motion)'. از رخش پای is a small prepositional object: از = 'from', رخش = the steed Rakhsh, پای = 'foot/hoof' — idiomatically 'to set the horse's foot moving'. Word order places the temporal adverb and verb before the prepositional phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s429-g45: به جایی شوم is a volitive/wishful construction: به = 'to', جایی = 'a place/somewhere', شوم = 1st‑person subjunctive/potential 'may I become / I will go'. کو نیاید نشان = 'where no sign/trace will come/appear' (کو = 'where', نیاید = negated form 'does not come/appear', نشان = 'trace/token'). The clause expresses a desire to be somewhere without a trace left.
segments.s429-g46: به = 'to/toward'. زابلستان‌گر here reflects an older poetic sequence where گر (if) or a formative is attached to Zabulistan; read idiomatically as 'toward Zabulistan / if (he) turns to Zabulistan'. کند = 3rd‑person singular present 'he does/makes'. سرفشان is the compound سر افشان ('head‑tossing' / dishevelled head) used adjectivally or as an epithet; the exact nuance is idiomatic/poetic here. The whole short clause therefore gives a conditional‑directional sense: 'if he turns toward Zabulistan, (he will be) head‑tossing.'
segments.s429-g47: سرانجام = 'in the end/ultimately'. ازان کار = 'from that affair/deed' (ازان = 'from that', کار = 'affair/deed' used as the object). سیر آید is an impersonal/resultative expression where سیر = 'satiety/satisfaction' and آید = 'comes' → 'satisfaction comes' i.e. 'he will be satisfied'. او is the third‑person pronoun placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s429-g48: اگرچه = 'although'. ز = poetic short form of از = 'from/because of'. بد = 'bad/evil' modifying the reason. سیر = 'satiety/satisfaction' again, دیر آید = 'comes late' (دیر = 'late', آید = 'comes'). او = 'he' (repeated for emphasis). The clause concedes that his becoming sated will be delayed because of the evil aspect.
segments.s429-g33: زدم is a 1st-person singular past verb ('I struck'). چند is an adverb here meaning 'several' and modifies the verb. بر + گبر اسفندیار is a prepositional phrase 'against the infidel Esfandiyar' (بر = against/on, followed by the noun + proper name). The phrase order is verb — adverb — prepositional object (poetic word-order).
segments.s429-g34: گراینده is a present‑participle/noun 'the one who turns/directs' and functions as the subject of this clause. دست مرا is the affected 'my hand' (مرا is the object clitic, here marking the person affected), داشت is a past-tense verb 'held/considered', and خوار is a predicative adjective 'contemptible/low'. Together داشت خوار (with مرا as the patient) means 'he regarded/treated me as contemptible.'
segments.s429-g35: همان تیغ من is 'that very sword of mine' (demonstrative + noun + possessor). گر (conditional particle) attached to من here introduces the condition ('if (you had seen) mine'); بدیدی is a past second-person form 'you had seen' (classical conditional sense). پلنگ is the object/topic carried into the result clause ('your panther'). Poetic word order can place the object before the verb of the next clause.
segments.s429-g36: نهان is an adverb/adjective 'hidden', داشتی is past 2nd‑person imperfect/past ('you would have kept/you had'), خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive 'yourself/itself' used for emphasis, and زیر سنگ is the prepositional phrase 'under a rock/stone' (زیر = under, سنگ = rock). Together: 'you would have hidden (it) yourself under a rock.'
segments.s429-g37: نبرد here functions as a locative/time noun 'in battle' (with همی adding continuity/emphasis: 'ever/in battle'). جوشن is 'the cuirass/armor'. اندر + برش is a fused locative phrase: اندر = 'in/within', and برش is the enclitic form indicating 'on him' (poetic compression of بر + ش). The verb 'was' is omitted (classical poetic ellipsis) so the clause reads 'in battle the armor [was] on him.'
segments.s429-g38: نه negates the clause ('not'). آن پاره پرنیان is 'that piece/strip of silken cloth' (آن = that, پاره = piece/strip, پرنیان = silken robe/cloth). بر سرش is 'on his head' (بر = on, سر = head, ش = his). The clause denies that the silken strip on his head was the (relevant) protection.
segments.s429-g39: سپاسم is an archaic/poetic formula 'my thanks' or 'I give thanks' (here functioning as 'I thank'). ز یزدان = 'to/from God' (poetic 'thanks to God'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/because': شب تیره شد = 'the night grew dark' (تیره + شد = became dark).
segments.s429-g40: دران = 'in that' (در + آن fused), تیرگی = 'darkness', چشم او = 'his eye' (چشم = eye, او = he/his), and خیره شد is a passive/resultative expression 'became dazed/was fixed/stared' (خیره شدن can mean to be stunned or to have one's gaze fixed). So: 'in that darkness his eye became dazed.'

sentences.s429-s25: Zāl said to him, “O son, give ear; when you bring these words to mind, keep your wits.”
sentences.s429-s26: All the affairs of the world have one door; except death — that has another door.
sentences.s429-s27: I know one remedy: choose this — that I summon the Simurgh as an ally for this.
sentences.s429-s28: If he becomes (our) guide because of this counsel, our realm, land, and home will remain to us.
sentences.s429-s9: Rostam said to him, “What good is sorrow over this? For this is a matter from heaven.”
sentences.s429-s10: There lies ahead a task that is more difficult, and my life is fuller of anxiety because of it.
sentences.s429-s11: Although I may make more excuses, (as if) to increase the radiance of this lion‑hearted one.
sentences.s429-s12: He will obtain nothing but utter misfortune in speech, in deed, and in rebellion.
sentences.s429-s13: I went to every quarter around the world; I learned news of both the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s429-s14: I seized the girdle of the white demon; I struck it on the ground like a single‑trunked willow.
sentences.s429-s15: I would not bow my head to Esfandiyar because of that strength and that forbearance in battle.
sentences.s429-s16: My dagger found passage from the anvil; you would have been humbled had you obtained a shield.
sentences.s429-s1: With that countenance Rostam reached the iwan; he showed/held out his hands in that manner.
sentences.s429-s2: Zovāreh and Faramarz began to weep; they were consumed (burned) by that weariness.
sentences.s429-s3: Rudabeh still lifts/arranges her hair from the head; at the sound of their voices her face grows weary.
sentences.s429-s4: Zovāreh quickly loosened/opened his middle (girdle); from him they drew forth the Babr-e Bayan (the famed sword).
sentences.s429-s5: Whoever was wise and came from his country — all of them at once sat down at his door.
sentences.s429-s6: He commanded that his steed be brought before him, and whoever was a desperate seeker of remedy...
sentences.s429-s7: With their honored hands they would pull at their hair, and, overcome by weariness, rub their faces.
sentences.s429-s8: He kept saying, 'I, still alive with a hoary head, have seen such an honored son.'
sentences.s429-s21: I have escaped from that dragon's claw; I do not know whether by this I have been freed of weariness.
sentences.s429-s22: What shall I think now? There is no counsel except this: that tomorrow I will set Rakhsh's foot in motion.
sentences.s429-s23: I will go to some place where no trace will come; if he turns toward Zabulistan, (he will be) head‑tossing.
sentences.s429-s24: In the end he will be sated with that affair, although because of its evil his satiation will come late.
sentences.s429-s17: I struck the infidel Esfandiyar several times; the one who turned (aside) held me in contempt.
sentences.s429-s18: Had you seen that very sword of mine, you would have hidden your panther under a rock.
sentences.s429-s19: In battle the cuirass was upon him; it was not that strip of silken robe on his head.
sentences.s429-s20: I thank God that the night grew dark; in that darkness his eye became dazed.
